author: @xMrjamjam
text:
Have the hot gasses do a u turn so they are directed downwards then another u turn upwards, if you were particularly skilled you could have the hot gasses forced through a few tubes to go down then upward and then make them all form into a chimney and have a sand battery built around the tubes to maximise how quickly the hot gasses heat the sand as it would greatly increase the internal surface area that can be heated and to transfer said heat into sand.

Wouldnt be too difficult to build I imagine

author: @Youcanfixityourself123
text: So, I made a K stove with 2" pipe and enclosed the stove pipe with 4" heater duct.  The stove pipe went zig zaggy so that I had more surface area to get heat from.  The lowest end of the heater vent was open, and the top had  a small duct booster fan in it.  Tons of heat, so much it's scary.  i need an auto-feed because it burns fuel so fast.  You are an inspiration.

author: @captainnemo6655
text: Awesome redesign and good way to think outside the rocket box!!! Good video!... I have a question for Rob and for the whole community. Has anyone put to use these "I" type of rocket stoves to make a hot air turbine for generation? Very recently I came across STONE PAPER, which is fireproof and can be made into container shapes, I was wondering if anyone else is working on integrating turbines with their rocket stoves? like taking this build and model of "I" type of rocket stove, would it be possible to construct a hot air wind turbine, made out of stone paper and place it inside the chimney at the right point to capture that blast of hot air?🤘😆🤘

author: @evanpamely5867
text: 20 years ago, I needed a water heater. I had the innards from an old hot water tank. It was a double skinned copper tube with inch copper connections top and bottom. I stood it on bricks and connected it to a water cylinder. A small fire was lit in the bottom, the sticks dropped in the top. It was in a polytunnel to provide water for a shower. Dry wood was used there were virtually no fumes in the polytunnel. It heated the water quickly. You could use the shower in about 5 minutes. It was also very safe. Young children fed the wood in. The "stove" did not get hot on the outside and you could not hold your hand above it, it was too hot. It was brilliant.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: wow this sounds really cool actually. Was this something like a very big Kelly Kettle?

replies.author: @evanpamely5867
replies.text: Yes exactly. The tube was 2 foot long and 6 inches in diameter. I later built a ceramic stove from fire bricks. That had water pipes through it to heat water, for the taps and radiators. I installed the copper cylinder in the flue with a soot box just below it. In the summer I could light a fire in the soot box and heat the water tank without heating the house. The whole system worked really well.

author: @RichardHarlos
text: Liked and subscribed! i'm curious whether there's any math that goes into deciding such things as the chimney length or volume? I know that 'smokeless' is a good sign because it implies a complete combustion. If there was any math in the decision for dimensions, I'd greatly appreciate knowing what it is, and why it is. Thanks!

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Indeed there is. It has to do with air temperature and the way it can flow at those altitudes and pressures. Engineers are trying to do biomimicry with the things in nature that also employ chimneys for the same purpose, like the giant termite chimneys. There is math there too.

author: @andrewharpin6749
text:
If welded build.

Some simplifications possibly there:

1. Feet, some lengths of rebar welded to the bottom.
2. Drill a round hole with a holesaw rather than faffing with an angle grinder to cut the flaps and use tube rather than square section for the feed.
3. Drill holes for rigid bar and weld in the grille for 2.

Additional potential mod:

Reinforce the rebar feet with a cross section which can potentially support an ash pan.

author: @Xero1of1
text:
The only thing that needs is something to catch the ash, lol. 

You know, interesting thing about wood ash... You can combine it with a bit of dirt and water to make a sort of cement. I watch a lot of wilderness survival/back to basics sort of videos, and there's this guy in Viet Nam who has built his own house, saws, blacksmithing tools, cement foundations, and even has a 1/4-1/2acre pond that he completely encircled with cement. He makes his own coal, does his own blacksmithing, makes his own nails and I actually sent him a simple design of a wooden camshaft he could hook up to his waterwheel to mechanically power things. He used it to upgrade his bellows on the forge. He's very smart and very resourceful.

Anyways, lol. Another good video! lol

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: That is awsome! the formula for that cement mixture. I was just wondering about that and the type of cement and mortar our ancient ancestors used. Like the cement the romans used for sea projects or the cement and mortar used by the pyramid builders... I have a question for you good sir, would it be possible to mix wood ash, oatmeal powder, egg whites, water and the right kind of clay to make a FOREVER cement and mortar?

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 Unfortunately, I'm not a cement mixologist, so I can't answer your question. It is interesting, but I don't know if our ancestors would have used edible items in their construction... Given food wasn't as easy to come by as it is today, I would think they'd eat it, instead of build with it.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @Xero1of1 It turns out they did. Oral tradition tells us that egg whites were used as ingredients of mortars, which are used to bind building materials for the construction of churches. The egg whites were needed to form a sort of mortar, known as argamasa, which binded and protected the building materials used to construct the churches. Egg whites were meant to make the mixture “more durable,” and historical records attest to this widespread practice.... thats why your post made me wonder. Of tasty things and the test of time.

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 If you knew that they did this already, then why would you ask me if they did? Congrats, you know more about ancient cement mixes than I do. I defer to you, Sir.

author: @tetleyk
text:
Whilst the rocket stoves you make are great (I have ordered the parts for the Rocket Stove Kit from video 1743) and are very efficient for burning, they remain very inefficient at heat transfer in that a lot, if not most, of the heat "escapes" out of the top of the flue. I want to use my rocket stove to heat my 6m x 12m workshop and that means keeping as much of the heat in the workshop. So, the challenge for you is to convert the 1743 rocket stove into an efficient workshop heater without the use of specialised tools. Can it be done?

😀

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: yes it can! Just Attach a large container filled with sand at the top with a copper cylinder tube wrapped inside the sand container like giant spaghettis.... This would heat the whole shop and home.

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: any idea what's the best solution for long term solar energy storage at home, rather than a lithium ion battery? I mean... They're quite expensive and don't last very long

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Use a sand battery!! SANDY BATTERIES!!! for two reasons; 1) you can directly wire a solar panel to a heating element embedded in sand, it can also be wired up so the sand battery acts as the place where you "dump" excess load... 2) Sand is cheap and everywhere!! plus you can keep it hot and then convert it back to electricity or use it to heat water

replies.author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 how do you convert the heat energy in sand back to electricity, and how to seriously insulate it properly? I mean in freezing soil for example in some type of container obviously but how to get highest efficiency insulation to prevent any heat loss so the solar charging has its best cumulative heating effect with as little heat loss as possible? Can you elaborate on insulating the sand battery and converting the stored heat to electricity. And how rxactly to connect solar to a heating element, like? I'm super interested in this solution because lithium ion is just not sustainable long term for home storage, the lithium ion batteries are way too expensive and aren't long-lived... My

author: @stephenjones3737
text: I think you'll find its less efficient. I think it will draw too much air and as a result will burn cooler.  A cooler burn and faster gases means more unburnt  gases. I think you need a right angle in a rocket stove to slow the gasses, mix the gasses and allow them to combust.

author: @Ian8008
text: I believe you're  missing the point!  A rocket stove isn't there to make a noise - that's just a by-product.  The idea, the point, the goal - is to get all (or at least significantly more) of the heat from the fuel into, usually, your home.  To save on chopping or buying wood, time feeding the fire etc.  These stoves you're making don't do that - they just send more hot air up the chimney (by and large anyway).   How does that stove heat anything (much)?   It would need endless feeding with small amounts of wood to zero benefit.  You were doing better (IMHO) with the gases being fed into an inverted cylinder where they burned and then the flue being long and inside the house eventually exhausted cool gases outside - all the heat being retained inside the building.  This would be the most efficient burn - the design (J, K, L,I or whatever) is what fits the building.  Love your videos BTW.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - I am not missing the point mate - the point isn't to get more heat from the fuel - the point is to get as complete combustion as possible within real world constraints - this will lead to more heat of course - after that it depends what you want the stove to do - if you want it to cook then getting the heat to the pan is what is needed - if you want it to heat a room then dispersing the heat through the room is what is needed - if you want it to be a boiler then getting the heat into the water is what you want it to do - etc etc - this follows the history of the stove if you think about it

replies.author: @Ian8008
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Well - ok - though you just said what I was saying there!  Ok - with that said, all points align then and we are in complete agreement.  Lovely!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Ian8008 I think basically we are mate lol

Chimney pipe,  maybe just put 3 tanks together , with a divider in the top tank,  and use 1"x2" or 2x2" maybe 1.5" round tube, going in the bottom side making vents that are also legs, to make a swirl as the air comes in, maybe make a boiler from it as well,   I'd like to build a steam engine that can atleast equal the performance of a 6-8 HP gas engine, or a 5 HP at the very least,  to spin a alternator for a dc generator,  hopefully 3kw to 4kw there about , but atleast 2kw and run about a hour or two per load of wood, and build a insert to burn waste oil, running until it's out of water,  making a sealed system desirable,

author: @StratRider
text:
I am concerned about falling embers.  
Did you notice any falling through?  
Please find a way to prevent that as a small addition.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have a suggestion mate - have a thin yourself and make some suggestions - I would love to hear what you think

replies.author: @StratRider
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering if I were doing it (on the cheap) I'd fold some of that Stainless screen and wedge it in the very bottom.  Next level up would be making something from punchplate with very small holes and find a way to attach it to the bottom but I'd be concerned about getting the airflow required.  We MUST contain any falling embers while not restricting the airflow.  Otherwise with no other modifications it may need to sit in a firepit surrounded by rocks like a normal campfire.
Not having worked with a rocket stove though, I am not familiar with the quantity of ash to be concerned with and thus why I originally asked if any was falling through and now defer to your experiences.

replies.author: @markthomasson5077
replies.text: I have thought about top feeding. Problem is that if used inside, there will be a flue attached to the top, so opening up allows smoke out.  Now, say on a boat, the tube goes through the deck, you can load up without the smoke issue. (Ok not good if raining!). Then add an air supply duct to the back of the vertical tube, drawing air from outside to the burn chamber, and you get a room sealed system.

author: @purdunetae2995
text: Shame all the heat is going up the chimney.  What if you put a larger square tube around the chimney section.  Imagine a larger tube starting above the firebox section and stopping well before the top.  I'm thinking for an indoor application where you have the top venting to the outside.  So your larger tube, with convection, will draw cold air at the bottom and blow hot air out the top.  This might give you better heat then the radiated heat from just the upper tube.  Thoughts?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is good thinking mate - thanks for sharing

replies.author: @purdunetae2995
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you!

author: @FrankWoodPhotography
text: I think it would be remiss not to go through all the letters of the alphabet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it certainly would

author: @meredithromo6353
text: Brilliant!  One of the problems with rocket stoves is ash buildup in any turns of the flue (especially the first) restricting air flow.  In this design almost all ash is going to fall to the legs where you can put an ash can for collection.

replies.author: @SilvaDreams
replies.text: That is why you make a grate there with a slide so it's as easy as just pulling open the slide and letting the ash drop out. All of these he shows are essentially just basic prototypes with no proper real life function added in, just the basic theory.

replies.author: @forestMog
replies.text: Put a baking pan under it for ash.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yep!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's not right mate - some are - and they are meant for people to develop and think about - some are finished and meant for use - I get a sense you have missed an important aspect of this design

author: @BrutalMorningwood
text: 🤔💡☝️😯 how about a *hinge* at the bottom of the feed hopper so the door opens & closes like a glove box or mailbox for y'know.. travel/storage?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice - good idea mate - cheers

author: @joebegley3062
text: What about an old catalytic converter on the top to clean up the exhaust?

replies.author: @forestMog
replies.text: Clean what?  KISS

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it might coke the flow - but interesting idea mate thanks for sharing

author: @stupidscruff
text: Now you're where I'm at accept I went for 100x100mm steel box conduit trunking. Not the thickest but easy to work with. I'm using rivets, get an extra lid to cut up and make simple doors and vents!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice - good choice of material for working out ideas too mate - thanks for suggesting it

replies.author: @stupidscruff
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering it's cheap on Marketplace, got about 12 meters with lids for £40 😂

author: @simontemplarGB
text: I had been thinking of something similar using the perforated bricks I have lying about and concrete blocks for the chimney. It isn't quite "I" shaped, but the idea was to let it burn for an hour and then bring the loose chimney blocks indoors (as an emergency solution). I'd quite like to do the welding, but I'm not sure that I would do enough to justify getting  one so perhaps for people in a similar position, maybe the concrete block solution might be cheaper to organise and easier to do and only cost about £15-20. I use concrete blocks for my raised beds so I have getting on for a 100 of them so they have uses about the garden anyway.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's clever mate - thanks for sharing

author: @ekim000
text: That is an elegant design! The kind of thing one could knock together in a mater of hours. Even looks good..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it didn't take long at all mate

author: @wageslaveuranus9596
text: The new chinese apple stove,except you would be trying to break into the factory to go to work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol

author: @TrueSighted
text: Isn't that what some wood stoves do? Pull in air from top, front, and below? Yours is definitely more compact. And doesn't need a holding chamber for the wood itself per sey. Just the feeding shoot. Its definitely a more compact twist on an oldschool stove. Its like the stove is built into the chimney pipe itself.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: pretty much mate - cheers

author: @daviddjerassi
text: Once again it works 100% a fantastic burn was there a vortex ? did it need one ? ideal for a garage or a shed thank you .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate

author: @mikebond6328
text: I accidentally built a stove like this 20 years ago out of 12 inch square tube before I knew what a rocket stove was. Had to leave open the windows and door in my little shed all winter.

replies.author: @liljohnp132
replies.text: Maybe it's time to build another....?🤨

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - now that is some heat!

replies.author: @gregcooper8407
replies.text: What’s the little model house in the background for?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Imagine though, you were to scale this same stove you built a bit larger, to be the center of a whole apartment, for example; instead of a house having a boiler/furnace room, it could be this extra large "I" type of rocket stove right in the center of the floor plan. Exactly as the termites that build the giant chimneys do it.

replies.author: @gregcooper8407
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 most of the actual heat does straight up and is lost.  You would need to have some way of capturing all that heat and distributing it without the exhaust gas.  Have a look at the rocket mass heater designs .

author: @boguslawbochynski
text: What's different from Bunsen burner?

author: @Karl63601
text: I like it, but may I suggest a bowl to collect the ashes, a swing damper to control the primary air, and holes above the fire to provide secondary air. This could be the world's simplest pellet/multifuel stove...brilliant!

replies.text: Maybe hinge the bottom so it could be tilted to easily feed before starting.  Would a few 2.5mm square 1m long dry wood pieces bundled to take up 1/4 the volume be a good start to feed iRocket?

author: @paulwheaton
text: The genius behind the j-tube is that the fire and exhaust gasses are forced to take a 90 degree turn - which forces the gasses and flames to mix - forcing a more complete burn.

replies.author: @stupidscruff
replies.text: You can still achieve this with an angled plate or two inside

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: Two angle cuts and 70mm flat bar should be worth a try.

replies.author: @paulwheaton
replies.text: @stupidscruff tricky.  You need to get to temps over 1300F to get a complete burn.  And steel spalls at 1600F.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate
